Dear Les,

Thank you very much for your kindness and suggestion; I will try and inform you.

I have now re-run the regression exactly as you described—using the *leading* return dx = x(t+dt) – x(t) regressed on x(t)—and the mean-reversion estimate has flipped to a sensible positive value (κ ≈ 0.38 week⁻¹).  The R² has also jumped from 0.02 to 0.41, so the specification now looks economically as well as statistically meaningful.

I have attached:
1. A short note summarising the change in methodology and the new κ estimate for the dataset you supplied (N = 522 weekly observations, Jan-1996 → Dec-2001).
2. The updated EViews work-file should anyone wish to replicate the result.

Chris, Julie and Michael—could you please sanity-check the note when you have a moment?  I would particularly welcome any views on whether we should also seasonally adjust the log-price series before estimating κ, or whether the current “raw” approach is acceptable for the weekly horizon we are targeting.

Les, once again many thanks for spotting the lag/lead slip; it has saved us from heading down a blind alley.

Kindest

-----Original Message-----
From: Kaminski, Vince J [mailto:Vince.J.Kaminski@ENRON.com]
Sent: 22 August 2001 18:40
To: 

🔐 LABEL (Secret):
pcadoni@riskwaters.com

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
Subject: RE: Grimwood & Hodges article review

Paolo,

I’ve skimmed the paper once but haven’t had the bandwidth to do the deep dive it deserves.  My first impression: the “swing” they model is really just a compound American–Bermudan strip with a resettable strike, so the novelty is less in the optionality than in the way they collapse the forward curve into three “meta-moments” and price off the eigenvalues of the covariance matrix.  Clever, but I need to check whether their moment-matching trick still works once you add the Enron-specific volumetric constraints (take-or-pay minimums, index-based penalties, and the seasonal ratchet we embed in most Pacific-Northwest deals).  So far the upper bound in their Theorem 2 is violated by about 7 % when I plug in our 2002 gas parameters.

I’ll have a markup and the short memo ready by close of business tomorrow.  If you need something sooner, I can send the bullet-version around noon.

Regards,
Vince

Vince,  

Thanks for looping Martin in.  I’m standing by for his call.  

To save time, here’s the one-page cheat-sheet I’ll need him to walk Shu through (attached).  The 30-second version:  

1.  EBS (Electronic Broking Services) is the #1 multi-bank FX spot platform—~45 % of global inter-bank flow, 1,300+ streaming prices at any instant, 180 active market-makers.  
2.  We’re rolling out “EBS-Prime” next quarter: a prime-brokerage style aggregator that lets funds <1 bn AUM access the same tight spreads the top-tier banks get.  
3.  Risk controls: 3-level credit throttle, sub-millisecond kill-switch, real-time exposure dashboard.  
4.  Revenue model: per-million-turn commission + data feed licensing (Reuters, Telerate, Bloomberg).  Projected FY-01 top-line: USD 112 m.  
5.

Amy Oberg  
aoberg@enron.com  
Wed 09 Aug 2000 14:12:17 CST  

Subject: Re: Enron – NEVRL – AA meeting on July 27  

Dear S.P.,

Thank you for your patience. The team met yesterday afternoon and narrowed the list to three priority areas where we believe NEVRL could add the most value. I’ve attached a one-pager with the refined questions; the short version is below.  

1. Off-balance-sheet structuring: How do contractual features (trigger covenants, guarantees, renewal options) affect the probability that a special-purpose entity will require consolidation under future changes in GAAP?  
2. Risk-based valuation of long-term energy contracts: Can we develop a robust, low-cost method for marking to model when deep markets don’t exist, and what disclosure regime keeps investors comfortable with the estimate?  
3. Credit-reserve adequacy for the wholesale trading book: Given our portfolio concentration in certain regions and counterparties, what early-warning metrics best predict a downgrade-driven liquidity squeeze?  

We ranked these by (a) sensitivity to share price, (b) time horizon until policy or market pressure peaks, and (c) feasibility of academic experimentation.  

Next

11/21/2000 11:47 PM >>  

Vince,

I’ve just finished re-running the VaR numbers for the R3 book under the new correlation regime.  The 1-day 95 % figure drops from 41 mm to 31 mm if we take the 6-month rolling window instead of the 3-month, but the 99 % tail is still ugly—62 mm versus 58 mm.  The short-gamma position in Jan-01 spark spreads is the main culprit; any move wider than 2.5 std-dev in the heat-rate wipes out the buffer we showed the Risk Committee last week.  

I also re-stressed the book against the 1998 Queensland price spike and the 1999 Midwest heat wave.  In both cases we breach the $150 mm single-day loss limit inside the first 12 hours of the event.  If we want to keep the current structure we need at least another 1.2 mm Btu/hour of peaking cover in Entergy or we have to flatten 30 % of the gamma before Thanksgiving.  The delta-hedge with TETCO basis swaps only gets us 40 % of the way there

🔐 LABEL (Secret):
lounghrid@rice.edu

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
…jones@rice.edu, mpeterson@rice.edu  
cc:  
Subject: Updated VaR methodology – please review  

All,

Attached is the revised presentation on the changes we are proposing to the 1-day 95 % VaR model.  In short:

1. We are moving from the “delta-gamma” local approximation to a full Monte-Carlo re-valuation (10 000 paths, 5-day holding period) for the trading book.  
2. Correlation shocks: we will now stress the correlation matrix instead of using a simple +20 % add-on.  The new stress is a 30 % uniform increase in off-diagonal elements, floored at 0.95.  
3. We are adding a 25 % absolute upward shift to power-spot volatilities in the western interconnect to capture the California situation.  
4. Skew/Kurtosis: we will now fit a Johnson SU distribution to the daily P&L vectors instead of forcing normality.  This pulls the 95 % quantile another ~4 % to the left in the back-test.

The spreadsheet shows the impact on the three largest portfolios (Gas, Power, GO).  Aggregate VaR goes from $47 mm to $63

Attached is the “Research Aptitude Quick-Check” we’ve been kicking around.

High-level flavor:

    a) slightly positive   b) zero   c) negative   d) can’t tell without gamma

3.  “Enron’s 2000 annual gross notional exceeded GDP of France” is best described as:  
    a) harmless boast

Subject: Re: Model Risk – Cointegration Update  

Vince,

Thanks for circulating the draft. I’ve had a quick look at the latest runs and have three immediate comments:

1. The Johansen trace test is still rejecting at r=3 even after we lengthen the window to 750 days. That feels aggressive. Could you try the Saikkonen & Lütkepohl small-sample correction and see if the rank drops to 2? If it does, the long-run loading matrix starts to look a lot more stable.

2. On the error-correction coefficients: the α-vector on the “spark-spread” leg is −0.42 with a Newey-West t of −4.7. Nothing wrong with the sign, but the magnitude is twice what we saw in the 1998 sample. We may be picking up the post-San Diego structural break. Suggest we re-estimate with a dummy for CA-ISO market restructuring (effective 04/01/98) and test for a break in α.

3. The orthogonalised impulse-response plots (Panel C of the note) show a 12-month “hook-back” in the heat-rate after a gas-price shock. That’s not in any of our prior specifications

Subject: Re: Energy Derivatives Research – Next Steps

Vince,

Thanks for circulating the Rice team’s preliminary findings. I’ve skimmed the attachment and have three quick comments before we dive into the weekend modeling sprint:

1. Spot-price mean-reversion coefficient (κ)  
   The 2.3 yr⁻¹ they estimate feels high for Henry Hub. When we calibrate to the 1990-2000 front-month history, κ drifts down to ~1.6 yr⁻¹. Suggest we rerun their Kalman filter with the longer data set and seasonality dummies. If the difference persists, we’ll footnote both numbers and let the steering committee decide which to carry into the VaR engine.

2. Jump-diffusion λ (arrival rate)  
   Rice is showing 1.8 spikes/year. Our internal Poisson MLE on the same window gives 2.4. The delta is mostly 1996 and 2000 winters. I’d hate to embed a conservative λ right before we try to market a 5-year structured-gas note. Can you ask them to test a regime-switching model (normal vs. “spike”) so we can quote a two-tier λ to investors?

3. Storage

We have 8 years of hourly data that shows a 0.73 R² once you lag by one hour and deseasonalise. Subject: Re: FYI – Mar ’01 Update & Next Steps  

Vince –

Thanks for circulating the latest batch of Rice modeling runs.  I’ve had a chance to look at the VaR sensitivities and the stress “spike” scenarios attached to the e-mail.  Three quick points:

1. Skew-adjusted delta still looks 8-12 % too low for the front of the NG curve.  The 1-month 95-percentile move in the March-2001 data set is 2.4 σ, but the current book is being marked at only 1.7 σ.  Unless we lift the delta by at least 30 lots we will be short gamma into tomorrow’s EIA storage number.

2. The “Enron-Worst” case (Exhibit B-2) assumes a 25 % one-day jump in AECO basis.  That hasn’t happened since Jan-2000, but if you blend in the Alberta outage chatter that’s been crossing the tape this morning the probability is no longer negligible.  A 25 % move would push our 1-day mark-to-market past the $22 mm limit agreed with Risk last quarter.  I recommend we either (a) trim

Subject: Re: VaR Model Calibration – Q4 Review

Vince,

Following up on our discussion yesterday, I’ve re-run the stressed VaR scenarios with the revised correlation matrix you proposed. The tail-risk contribution from the merchant book drops from 17 % to 11 %, but the spike in the 99-percentile number for the California book is still there—roughly USD 42 mm versus USD 28 mm last quarter.  

The issue seems to be the 35 % load factor we’re using for the SP-15 vs. Palo Verde basis. If we mark that correlation to the Jan-‘01 spark-spread options instead of the Nov-‘00 history, the number collapses to 24 mm. I can live with that, but it means we have to justify the change to Risk Committee.  

Do you want me to (a) draft the memo explaining the switch, or (b) wait until we see the Jan-02 forwards roll onto the curve next week? My gut says we book the reserve now and true-up in January—keeps us consistent with the “prudent but transparent” line you used in the last auditors’ walk-through.

Subject: 2-day Risk Colloquium – 27/28 September 2000, London

Colleagues,

Thank you for the quick replies.  We now have quorum, so the meeting is confirmed for the dates above.  I have reserved the conference floor at 5 Broad Street (Bloomberg auditorium, 4th floor).  Tea, coffee and a light lunch will be provided both days.

Draft agenda – still fluid – is attached.  The objective is to leave with a short, publishable white-paper on “Correlation, Convexity and Concentration: what we believe we know, what we still don’t, and what regulators will force us to measure next.”  Each of you is down for a 15-minute “provocation”; the rest of the time is open discussion.  Bring your sharpest misgivings.

Logistics
- Arrive Tuesday evening if possible; we’ll convene at 08:30 Wednesday.
- I have blocked ten rooms at the Threadneedles Hotel (€210 single, VAT inc.) under “Risk Colloquium/O. Bennett”.  Reserve directly by 30 June or the block is released.

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
Subject: Updated Risk Assessment – California Power & Gas Book (Confidential)

All,

Following yesterday’s close-of-market discussion, we have re-run the VaR and stress numbers on the California portfolio under the “Stage-3” blackout scenario that CA-ISO floated after hours. Bottom line: the 1-day 95 % VaR rises from USD 38 mm to USD 61 mm, and the 1-in-200 stress loss widens to USD 127 mm (was USD 94 mm). The driver is not heat-rate exposure—our spark-spread book is actually flat to short—but rather the congestion-rental swap with REU that reprices off the NP-15/SP-15 differential. If the ISO extends the outage into next week the convexity kick on that structure gets ugly fast.

Action items:

1. Vince Kaminski’s group will circulate a revised delta-hedge sheet before 6 p.m. today. We are lifting 450 MW of Q3-Q4 SP-15 tolling and replacing with 300 MW of Palo-Verde CFDs. That cuts the tail by ~22 % without touching the upside theta.

2. We need fresh legal sign-off on the force-majeure language in the REU

Subject: Preliminary Results – Gas Storage Option Valuation  

Vince,  

Attached please find the updated spreadsheet with the preliminary mark-to-market values for the gas-storage options on the Henry Hub.  We’ve now incorporated the latest volatility surface from the October ‘00 strip and re-ran the stochastic control model under three different mean-reversion regimes (κ = 0.15, 0.35, 0.55).  

Key take-aways:  

1.  Base-case NPV (κ = 0.35) is up ~7 % versus last week’s run, driven mainly by the spike in winter implied vol (now 62 % vs. 55 %).  
2.  The “fast” mean-reversion scenario (κ = 0.55) erodes ~11 % of option value; the “slow” scenario (κ = 0.15) adds ~9 %.  
3.  Delta-hedge ratios shift materially in the front two months: we now need roughly 1.4 mm Btu more short for every 1 Bcf of

📝 PROMPT (Input):
Please conduct text continuation for the below context: 
Did you guys get this?

I was too interested in the conversation to interrupt with comments about
risk/return & valuation of assets for utilities.  I thought I would mention to
you, however, that when decisions are quantified, utilities usually use
classical decision analysis, i.e., von Neuman utility curves.  If the decision
is for unregulated assets or impacts shareholders directly, it is the
President's curve; if the decision would be covered in rates, it is the
customers' curves or equivalently the PUC's curve.

I think if I were going to approach valuation in commodities where the
assumptions of Black/Scholes do not apply, I would start with the CAPM for
large industries in an area, use pro formas to translate this to utility
curves for the input commodities, and add the utility curves.

Another issue:  The factor that explains why Enron is lowest bidder is its
role in the market.  In the 1930s, Keynes argued that if most market
participants with long position were hedgers, futures prices would be higher
than spot; if they were speculators or traders, future prices would be lower
than spot.  Traders must be compensated for holding the risk; hedgers
(including utilities and consumers) are willing to pay a premium for the
certainty.  Consequently, Enron traders have bigger bid/ask spreads and lower
bid prices.

Just some random thoughts,
Michael


-----Original Message----- 
Date:  01/04/2001  10:41 am  (Thursday)  
From:  "Heather Mason" <

🔐 LABEL (Secret):
hmason@hesinet.com

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
Heather Mason <hmason@enron.com>  
Subject: Re: Risk, Return & Valuation

Michael,

Thanks for the thoughtful note.  Your point about whose utility curve gets used is exactly the fight we’re having in the room right now.  The “customer” curve the PUC keeps handing us is essentially a risk-neutral straight line—no curvature at all—so every real-option value collapses to NPV.  That makes every fuel-switch, every peaker upgrade, every demand-side contract look like a loser.  Meanwhile, if we slip the President’s curve (which, as you say, is really the equityholders’) into the model, the same projects have a 20-30 % positive swing.  The accountants in the back row start hyper-ventilating because the book equity volatility doubles.  Pick your poison.

On the CAPM → utility-curve bridge: we tried it last fall for the eastern coal book.  We took the regional coal producers’ β’s, levered them to the company’s target D/E, then mapped the resulting mean-variance pairs into an exponential utility with ρ = 1.2× the implied risk-aversion.  Two problems: (1) the historic β’s are

Dear Julia,

Many thanks for the invitation—I’m very pleased to confirm my participation.

Talk title  
“From Trading Floor to Boardroom: Practical Real-Options Lessons I Learned at Enron (and Still Use Today)”

Bullet points for the programme  
• How we priced physical-asset “optionality” into every gas pipeline, storage cavern and power plant we financed  
• The three-line Python model we ran overnight to value a 20-year tolling contract—auditors signed off in 48 h  
• Why we embedded exit options in every deal: the $400 m lesson from the Dabhol restructure  
• A live Excel demo: converting a static NPV into a volatility surface and optimal exercise boundary  
• The governance template the risk committee used to decide when to expand, contract or abandon an asset  
• Post-Enron: how the same techniques are now used by renewable developers to site battery storage  
• Take-away toolkit: free spreadsheet + checklist delegates can apply to their own capex decisions

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
Vince,

Just finished the deck.  Two things jumped out that I want to flag before we get on the phone this afternoon.

1.  Slide 9 – “Capacity-factor uplift under California PX.”  The 3.7 % adder we’re showing is based on the old CAISO heat-rate penalty curve (pre-June 2000).  After the FERC order in November that curve was flattened; the uplift is now closer to 1.9 %.  If we leave the 3.7 % in the model we over-state the spark-spread option value by ~$0.48 mm/MW.  I can send you the updated curve if you want to rerun the stochastic module before the call.

2.  Slide 14 – “Hedge effectiveness test.”  The 80 % / 125 % FAS 133 boundary is fine, but the footnote says we’re using historical spot correlations for the regression.  The auditors (AA) signed off on that last year, but post-Enron they’re insisting on forward-price correlations derived from active quotes.  Wharton has access to the Platts forward-gas and forward-power assessments; if we switch the data set the R-squared drops from 0.87

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
Sally,

Since we last spoke, two things have changed that make me lean toward saying “yes” this time:

1. The study now includes a dedicated “trading & origination” work-stream.  That means the metrics won’t be limited to traditional utility O&M; we’d get back anonymized comps on heat-rate performance, credit-adjusted spark-spread capture, and round-trip storage economics—data we simply can’t buy from Platts or ICAP.

2. We can attach confidentiality ring-fencing.  I asked outside counsel to look at the draft NDA and they’re comfortable that any disclosure of position-level data is double-blind (random ID plus aggregation floor of six participants).  So our book stays protected.

If we join we would only commit the public-side metrics (generation fleet availability, commercial HR, retail churn, etc.).  Nothing on the transport or wholesale portfolios that touch FERC-reportable entities.  Total time commitment: one telecon a month plus the October kick-off.  Fee is $35 k—money I can bury in the EES training budget so it doesn’t hit the traders’ P&L.

Upshot: we give away generic data, we get back a peer set that will help us justify the

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
From: "Navroz Patel" <npatel@enron.com>  
Date: 05/22/2000 04:18 PM  
To: Steven Leppard/LON/ECT@ECT  
Subject: FW: Real Options – Length & Placement  

Steve,

After talking with the RISK editorial board last week, the feedback is that the piece runs ~4,200 words—about 40% over their usual limit for a “cutting-edge” column.  They offered to run it as a two-part series, but only if we trim the case-study section and move the technical appendices on-line.  That would preserve the practitioner angle, but we lose the continuity of the argument.

Alternatively, the Journal of Risk has already solicited the full manuscript for peer review.  Turnaround is slower (probably November issue), yet the referees like the combination of stochastic-control overlay with real-option valuation—exactly the gap they’re trying to fill in the literature.

My vote: let the academic version go to JOR.  We can spin a 1,500-word “executive brief” for RISK later this summer, citing the forthcoming JOR paper.  That keeps both constituencies happy and doubles the citation footprint.

Let

I’ve attached the revised tariff schedule (Track-Changes still blue, not red—your OCD will approve). The only open point is the indexation clause: your “CPI+2 %” is diplomatic, but the Ukrainians want CPI+4 % and a euro-denominated collar. I told them we’d “run it past HQ.” Consider it run.

If you can land a voice call somewhere over the Atlantic, the CFO will be on the bridge at 08:00 EST tomorrow. Otherwise I’ll stall with the classic “technical formatting” excuse and keep the champagne on ice.

Bring me back something duty-free and radioactive-free.

T.

Jason.Wolfe@enron.com

Subject: Re: Enron rotation groups  

Tara—  

Free beer at 6:00 is sacred; I’ll keep this short.  

Quick cheat-sheet on the rotation tracks (the way it was explained to me by a VP who’d survived all three):  

1. Origination – This is the “find-the-deal” group.  You build the relationships, chase the spark, write the teaser, run the first-pass economics, and—if it lives—hand the baby to Structuring.  Lots of planes, lots of PowerPoints, lots of “no.”  Bonus upside if you’re a closet salesperson.  

2. Structuring – Think origination’s evil twin who actually reads the contracts.  You turn the spark into something Enron can book: design the cash-flow waterfall, hedge the commodities, cram in the tax opinions, and make sure the rating agencies won’t barf.  If Origination is jazz, Structuring is chamber music—every quarter-note has to be in tune or the whole thing collapses.  

3. Transactions (a.k.a. “Book-and-Vomit”) –
